retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Pathophysiology -- Swyer Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Swyer syndrome occurs due to reduced production of testosterone and AMH leading to a failure of gonadal differentiation. [122] Particular mutations associated with Swyer syndrome include mutations in NR5A1 (which encodes for SF1), WNT4, and the SRY gene, which mutates in 10 to 15% of patients with Swyer syndrome. [124] [123]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Genitourinary -- Pathophysiology -- Genital System
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, XY female gonadal dysgenesis (Swyer syndrome) results from point mutations or deletions of the SRY gene and will present as in females as a lack of menstruation and/or secondary sex characteristics at puberty. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Pathophysiology -- Swyer Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Swyer syndrome, also known as pure gonadal dysgenesis, occurs when there is abnormal sexual differentiation during embryological development, leading to insufficient intrauterine virilization with undifferentiated gonads with associated female phenotype. [122] The external genitalia is non-ambiguous and well-formed; however, the gonads are dysgenic and improperly formed. [123] Patients with Swyer syndrome have an XY 46 karyotype.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Testicle -- Clinical Significance -- Swyer Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Swyer syndrome has an incidence of 1 in 80,0000. [122] Swyer syndrome patients present with an above average height, underdeveloped breasts with normal axillary/pubic hair. The external genitalia is distinctly female; however, the uterus is small. The underdeveloped gonads are dysgenetic strips that do not have any endocrinological or reproductive function. [122] Patients with Swyer syndrome have an increased incidence of gonadal tumors such as gonadoblastomas or dysgerminomas. [122] One-third of patients with Swyer syndrome may suffer from dysgerminomas. [122]

retrieved_docs.title: Turner Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Ovarian Failure
retrieved_docs.content: All counseling and follow-up related to fertility and pregnancy should be conducted in specialized centers by an interprofessional team. This team should include endocrinologists, cardiologists, fertility experts, and clinicians with experience in the comprehensive care of patients with Turner syndrome. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the etiology of Asherman syndrome. Summarize the American Fertility Society classification of the severity of Asherman syndrome. Review the treatment considerations for patients with Asherman syndrome. Explain the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team to routinely monitor the endometrium for evidence of endometrial cancer which will improve outcomes in those with Asherman syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Young Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Impaired fertility: Semen analysis should be offered to the patient. In-vitro fertilization techniques, principally intracytoplasmic sperm injection, have been shown effective in this setting. Male patients with Young syndrome might present with infertility secondary to obstructive azoospermia. Followings are step-wise surgical management to address infertility in these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadal dysgenesis is a genetic condition due to errors in cell division and or alterations in genetic material, leading to complete or partial loss of gonadal development. The development of gonadal dysgenesis begins early either at fertilization or shortly after in the early stages of the embryo and fetus. Complete gonadal dysgenesis genotypes are known as either 46, XX or 46, XY. These patients with full gonadal dysgenesis exhibit streak gonads, female external genitalia, and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with associated amenorrhea and lack of secondary sexual characteristics but none of the other features of Turner syndrome described below [1] . The 46, XX type may develop due to genetic mutations or environmental factors affecting ovarian development leading to ineffective ovaries. 46, XX type gonadal dysgenesis is on the spectrum of conditions that cause premature ovarian failure. The XY type of complete gonadal dysgenesis, also called Swyer syndrome, typically develops due to mutations of genes in the SRY sex-determining region of the Y chromosome; multiple possible genes have been implicated here, but in many of the individual cases, an exact cause is unknown. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Turner Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Gaps remain in understanding the genetic basis of various comorbidities and the optimal treatment strategies. However, the use of hGH, hormone replacement therapy, structured cardiac monitoring and interventions, fertility assistance, enhanced psychosocial support, and improved management of other comorbidities have significantly improved the quality of life for individuals with Turner syndrome in recent decades (please refer to the Evaluation and Treatment / Management sections for more information). [21]

retrieved_docs.title: 17-Hydroxylase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Sex Hormone Replacement and Surgical Interventions in 46,XY Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Reproductive medicine specialists may consider additional treatment options for fertility, as many of these patients are unable to conceive. Historically, live births in these patients have been rare, but recent cases of successful live births have increased, primarily through assisted reproductive techniques and, in very rare instances, spontaneously after adequate disease control. These successful cases have utilized various protocols, including progesterone-primed ovarian stimulation, long-acting gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist/dexamethasone or prednisolone, short-acting GnRH agonist/dexamethasone, prednisolone, and ultra-long GnRH agonists. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Kallmann Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Although most patients seeking fertility assistance have a good response to hormonal therapy, some may not tolerate the treatment or may remain infertile despite aggressive therapy. In such cases, assisted reproduction techniques can be successfully used. Preimplantation genetic testing can be used to eliminate the disorder from any progeny, especially as next-generation gene sequencing technology advances to allow for easier and earlier diagnosis of the syndrome. [6] [67] Please see StatPearls' companion references, " Female Infertility ," " Male Infertility ," " Male Hypogonadism ," " Androgen Replacement ," and " Hormone Replacement Therapy ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Turner Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Common complications include infertility, congenital heart disease, hypertension, and autoimmune conditions. Favorable outcomes may be achieved through early diagnosis and comprehensive medical care, although long-term follow-up is generally necessary to monitor for associated health issues.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Gonadal Dysgenesis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In conclusion, multidisciplinary care involving a wide range of medical and allied specialties is required to provide optimal care to patients with gonadal dysgenesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobs Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Families who receive a prenatal diagnosis of 47,XYY syndrome should receive genetic counseling to aid in their understanding of the disease. Treatment of this syndrome is generally only supportive, with attention given to the comorbidities of the patient. Boys who are diagnosed with Jacobs syndrome may benefit from speech therapy and behavioral interventions from qualified professionals. Occupational therapy may be needed if hypotonia is present. Patients may benefit from supplemental or special educational resources if learning disabilities exist. Appropriate medical treatment for any comorbid condition, such as asthma or seizure disorder, should be elicited from a licensed clinician. Men with infertility should receive a comprehensive evaluation from a qualified reproductive endocrinologist. Hormonal imbalances, if found, may need to be corrected. Patients who are having difficulty achieving pregnancy due to low sperm counts may need to undergo in-vitro fertilization or intracytoplasmic sperm injection. [2] Many who do so are able to father children successfully with these methods. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Amenorrhea -- Etiology -- Ovarian Dysfunction
retrieved_docs.content: Primary amenorrhea due to ovarian dysfunction is most frequently a result of gonadal dysgenesis, which can be seen with Turner syndrome 45,X karyotype, or in patients with mosaicism. Less commonly, gonadal dysfunction can also be seen in patients with pure gonadal dysgenesis with either 46,XX or 46,XY karyotypes, the latter known as Swyer syndrome. Patients with Swyer syndrome have nonfunctional testicular tissue; therefore, they do not secrete antimüllerian hormone (AMH) or testosterone in utero. The lack of AMH allows the müllerian ducts to persist and develop into typical internal female reproductive structures, including the fallopian tubes, uterus, cervix, and upper vagina. The lack of testosterone also causes the external genitalia to develop as female. Therefore, these patients develop typical internal and external female genital structures but fail to develop secondary sex characteristics at puberty because their gonads are nonfunctional.

retrieved_docs.title: Amenorrhea -- Etiology -- Gonadal Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: Primary amenorrhea is also frequently due to gonadal dysgenesis or, rarely, enzymatic impairment (eg, 17α-hydroxylase or aromatase deficiency). Gonadal dysgenesis is often seen in patients with the 45,X karyotype (ie, Turner syndrome) or mosaicism. [6] Less commonly, it can present as pure gonadal dysgenesis in individuals with either a 46,XX or 46,XY karyotype (ie, Swyer syndrome). Patients with Swyer syndrome develop a uterus, fallopian tubes, and upper vagina, as well as external female genitalia, due to the lack of testicular hormone secretion in utero. People with gonadal dysgenesis have nonfunctional gonadal tissue, so they often present in early adolescents with absent pubertal development. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي) are interconnected medical conditions. Prevention and control measures are applicable to both female infertility and endometrial implantation, and the two conditions are related, indicating that endometrial implantation can contribute to or be associated with female infertility and that managing endometrial pathology is important in the care of women with infertility.

Collectively, the integrated knowledge of urogenital anatomy, renal and prostate pathology, the full spectrum of female reproductive disorders, laparoscopic and peritoneal surgical techniques, extracorporeal lithotripsy, sexual dysfunction categories, and targeted pharmacologic therapies illustrates a comprehensive framework for patient assessment and multidisciplinary care.

retrieved_docs.content: and medical procedures underscores the interwoven nature of urogenital and female reproductive health and supports a comprehensive, patient‑centered approach to diagnosis, treatment planning, and counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

The infectious process is identified as the underlying mechanism of post‑abortion complications, linking infection directly to adverse outcomes after termination procedures. Collectively, this structured framework interrelates prenatal surveillance, invasive diagnostics, therapeutic interventions, delivery assistance, placental and ectopic‑pregnancy management, and post‑delivery care, providing a comprehensive reference for clinical documentation, education, and patient‑centered obstetric management.

Collectively, the integrated hierarchy links abnormal findings, sexual‑dysfunction categories, urogenital and female reproductive system pathologies, obstetric complications and targeted ultrasound diagnostics, supporting comprehensive assessment, coordinated patient care and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are both hormonal agents that may help prevent إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In contrast, a history of CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY is clinically associated with an increased likelihood of إجهاض تلقائي, indicating that prior fetal structural anomalies can be a risk factor for subsequent spontaneous loss. Understanding that 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone can be used prophylactically, while recognizing the heightened risk linked to previous congenital malformations, is important for optimizing patient counseling and management strategies aimed at reducing the incidence of spontaneous abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2692
retrieved_docs.content: Radiotherapy is recognized as an allowable therapeutic option for both female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) and uterine endometriosis (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). These two gynecologic conditions are interrelated, as uterine endometriosis is a known contributor to infertility in women. Consequently, radiotherapy may be considered within the broader management strategies for these linked disorders, reflecting their clinical connection and the potential role of radiotherapeutic interventions in addressing both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 138
retrieved_docs.content: المخاض). Within the broader context of natural pregnancy cases and outcomes (حالات الحمل الطبيعي ونتائجه (SMQ)), cervical dilation and miscarriage (الكَلَف) represent normal and adverse events. The integrated concepts emphasize how uterine function, pelvic anatomy, fetal presentation, and genital‑tract disorders intersect to influence infertility assessment, labor progression, and the management of high‑risk obstetric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1698
retrieved_docs.content: Female Reproductive System Disorder is classified as a subcategory of other complications of pregnancy and also as a subcategory of اضطراب الحمل (pregnancy disorder). Both the broader category other complications of pregnancy and the specific disorder are anatomically associated with the ženský reprodukční systém (female reproductive system). This reflects that disorders affecting the female reproductive system are considered part of the spectrum of pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1719
retrieved_docs.content: Infertility in women of cervical origin is classified as a specific type of cervical disorder (اضطراب في عنق الرحم). This condition is a subset of the broader category of female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث). Female infertility is closely related to endometrial implantation abnormalities (انتباذ بطاني رحمي), indicating that disturbances in the uterine lining can contribute to the inability to conceive. Radiotherapy (radioterapie) is recognized as an approved therapeutic option for cervical disorders and for endometrial implantation abnormalities; in certain clinical scenarios where these pathologies underlie female infertility, radiotherapy may be considered as part of the management strategy. Understanding the hierarchical relationship between cervical‑origin infertility, general female infertility, and endometrial implantation, as well as the role of radiotherapy, helps clinicians tailor comprehensive treatment plans for patients experiencing infertility.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Female infertility (العقم لدى الإناث) is closely related to endometrial implantation (انتباذ بطاني رحمي). Abnormalities in the process of endometrial implantation can impair the uterine lining’s ability to support embryo attachment, thereby contributing to infertility in women. Recognizing the connection between these two conditions is important for clinicians when evaluating reproductive health and planning appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 992
retrieved_docs.content: This integrated framework aids clinicians in understanding the interconnections among various gynecologic conditions, facilitating more precise diagnosis, classification, and management of patients with disorders of the female reproductive tract.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 4 -- Community 2764
retrieved_docs.content: Endometriosis is a gynecological disorder characterized by the presence of endometrial‑like tissue that is normally located in the uterus, and its ectopic growth can cause pain, inflammation, and fertility issues. Understanding that endometriosis originates from tissue associated with the uterus is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted treatment, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2166
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically linked to several obstetric interventions and outcomes. It often leads to the use of labor induction (تحفيز المخاض) to initiate delivery when medically indicated, and in some cases delivery may be performed with forceps (الولادة بالملقط) to assist the birth process. Although intrauterine pregnancy itself signifies successful conception, certain underlying conditions or complications associated with the pregnancy can also be related to subsequent infertility (العقم), highlighting the need for careful evaluation of reproductive health before and after pregnancy. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians anticipate appropriate labor management strategies and address potential fertility concerns in patient care.
